_comment: V1.1 vector_filter msgpack fixture — canonical Filter::VectorSimilarity wire bytes (Rust rmp_serde::to_vec_named). The TS client (@msgpack/msgpack.encode) produces SEMANTICALLY EQUIVALENT bytes (decode-and-compare, not byte-identical). See tests/vector_filter_msgpack.rs.
_parity_note: Byte-IDENTICAL parity is not achievable for f32 fields: rmp_serde always emits float32 (0xca prefix) for f32-typed fields, while @msgpack/msgpack emits positive-fixint for integer-valued floats (1.0 → 0x01, 0.0 → 0x00, 2.0 → 0x02) AND float64 (0xcb prefix) for non-integer floats (0.5 → 0xcb…, not 0xca…). So choosing non-integer fixture values would NOT rescue byte-identity either. Both encodings are msgpack-valid and mutually decodable. The TS parity test (vector_filter_parity.test.ts) decodes both sides and deep-compares the decoded objects, proving semantic equality + mutual decodability.
_key_order_note: rmp_serde::to_vec_named emits map keys in Rust STRUCT DECLARATION order: op, field, query, k, ef_search, oversample. The Filter enum is #[serde(tag = "op", rename_all = "snake_case")], so the discriminator 'vector_similarity' is first. skip_serializing_if = Option::is_none means ef_search/oversample keys are ABSENT when None (not null). @msgpack/msgpack.encode preserves JS object insertion order — the TS builder constructs the object in the same order (op, field, query, k, then ef_search/oversample via the opts arg).

_value_notes.float32: All f32 values are serialized as msgpack float32 (0xca prefix): 1.0=ca3f800000, 0.0=ca00000000, 0.5=ca3f000000, 2.0=ca40000000. rmp_serde serializes f32 as float32 (not float64) because the Rust field type is f32.
_value_notes.ef_search_400: cd0190 = uint16 400 (0x0190). msgpack uses the smallest int encoding: values 128-65535 are uint16 (0xcd prefix).
_value_notes.k_10: 0a = positive fixint 10 (values 0-127 use a single byte).
_value_notes.map_headers: 84=fixmap4, 85=fixmap5, 86=fixmap6 — the prefix byte encodes the entry count (4/5/6 entries).
